1:
Ps 75: Asaph's Judgment Psalm — 'Do Not Destroy.' The superscription 'Al-Tashcheth' (do not destroy) is a tune designation shared with Pss 57, 58, 59 — possibly the opening words of a known song to whose melody this psalm was sung, or a liturgical instruction against destroying the psalm's content. The Asaph guild (Pss 50, 73-83) were Levitical singers with a distinctive theological tradition emphasizing divine sovereignty over history.

'We give thanks to you, O God; we give thanks, for your name is near. People recount your wondrous deeds' — the double hodinu (we give thanks) opens the psalm in the register of communal gratitude. 'Your name is near' (qarov shemechah) — divine nearness as the occasion of praise: the name is near because YHWH has acted within history in a way that can be reported (sippur niphleotechah, the recounting of wondrous deeds). The community's ongoing testimony of divine acts is the expression of the nearness of the divine name.
2: 'At the set time that I appoint I will judge with equity' — YHWH himself speaks: moedim (set times, appointed times — the same word for the covenant feast calendar). Divine judgment operates on a fixed schedule known only to YHWH. The 'set time' resonates with the prophetic tradition of et (appointed time): Habakkuk 2:3 ('the vision awaits its appointed time'), Daniel 8:19, Psalms 11:27, 35 — the eschatological calendar is fixed. The equity (misharin) of the judgment addresses the lament that the wicked currently prosper: the judgment is coming and will be perfectly proportioned.
3: 'When the earth totters, and all its inhabitants, it is I who keep steady its pillars' — the ANE cosmological image of the earth resting on pillars (ammudim) — the columns that support the flat earth above the cosmic waters. The earth 'tottering' in crisis is the dissolution of cosmic order, which the Psalter repeatedly associates with moral chaos (Psalms 82:5). YHWH as the one who holds the earth's pillars steady is the creator maintaining cosmic stability against the entropy of human wickedness. Cf. 1 Samuel 2:8 (Hannah's song: YHWH 'set the world on its pillars').
4: 'I say to the boastful, Do not boast, and to the wicked, Do not lift up your horn' — the divine speech continues. The 'horn' (qeren) in ANE iconography was the symbol of power and pride: warrior-kings wore horn-shaped helmets; the bull's horn was the emblem of strength. 'Lifting the horn' (rum qeren) is the physical gesture of aggressive self-assertion. YHWH forbids it to the wicked — the divine speech directly confronts the arrogance of the powerful who have forgotten the divine judgment of v2.
5: 'Do not lift up your horn on high, or speak with haughty neck' — 'with haughty neck' (betzavar ataq) — the lifted neck as the physical posture of arrogance: the head thrown back, the chin raised, looking down on others. This is the body language of the powerful oppressor in ANE relief art (kings trampling defeated enemies). The divine speech explicitly names and forbids the physical posture of pride.
6: 'For not from the east or from the west and not from the wilderness comes lifting up' — the geographic disclaimer: promotion/exaltation (harim, the lifting-up word of v4-5) does not originate from any point of the compass. 'East' (meitze), 'west' (umimarav), 'wilderness/south' (umimidbar) — the three geographic directions standing for the totality of human sources of power. Human exaltation comes from nowhere human — neither imperial power (east = Mesopotamia/Persia), maritime power (west = Mediterranean/Egypt), nor nomadic military power (south = wilderness).
7: 'But it is God who executes judgment, putting down one and lifting up another' — Elohim shofet zeh yashpil vzeh yarum — the central theological claim: YHWH alone controls the rise and fall of powers. This is the key text for the Psalter's theodicy: the wicked prosper and the righteous suffer only provisionally; YHWH is the sovereign who ultimately determines which direction the trajectory goes. Daniel's theology of divine sovereignty over kingdoms (Daniel 2:21: 'He deposes kings and raises up kings') derives from this tradition.
8: 'For in the hand of the LORD there is a cup with foaming wine, well mixed, and he pours out from it, and all the wicked of the earth shall drain it down to the dregs' — the cup of divine wrath is one of the most pervasive ANE and biblical images: YHWH's judgment administered as a cup of wine that produces reeling, stupor, and collapse. The cup appears in Jeremiah 25:15-17 (YHWH's cup poured to all nations), Ezekiel 23:32-34 (Oholibah's cup of judgment), Habakkuk 2:16 (shame-cup), Isaiah 51:17-22 (Jerusalem's cup of staggering), and Revelation 14:10; Psalms 16:19 (the cup of God's wrath). The dregs (shmarim) were the sediment at the bottom of the wine — the worst, most concentrated portion, which the wicked must consume entirely.
9: 'But I will declare it forever; I will sing praises to the God of Jacob' — the psalmist's own vow: the declaration of divine judgment becomes the content of perpetual praise. The 'God of Jacob' — the covenant-identification: not the God of abstract philosophy but the God who entered covenant with a specific person (Jacob) and his descendants. The cup of wrath for the wicked is the same God's covenant faithfulness to Jacob's children.
10: 'All the horns of the wicked I will cut off, but the horns of the righteous shall be lifted up' — the reversal that closes the psalm: the wicked's horn-lifting (vv4-5) is cut off; the righteous person's horn is lifted by divine action. The same horn-imagery — now the divine response completes the contrast. The righteous are not self-promoted (the warning of vv4-5) but exalted by YHWH. This is the pattern of Psalms 113:7-8 and Hannah's song (1 Samuel 2:4-8): divine reversal of human status hierarchies.
